children.name: Lichenostomus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with brush-tipped tongues adapted for nectar feeding, found primarily in Australia and New Guinea. They are characterised by their elegant, slightly decurved bills and often feature striking facial markings, such as ear-tufts and eye-rings. They play a crucial role as pollinators of many Australian flowering plants.

children.name: Lichenostomus melanops
children.description: A striking honeyeater with a yellow crown, bright yellow ear-tufts, and a black facial mask, found in eucalypt forests and woodlands of eastern Australia. It feeds primarily on nectar but also takes insects and fruit, often aggressively defending flowering trees from other birds. Its rich, musical song is a distinctive sound of Australian forests.

children.name: Anthochaera
children.description: The wattlebirds — a genus of large, aggressive honeyeaters named for the fleshy wattles hanging from their cheeks or throats. They are among the largest members of the honeyeater family, with powerful bills and strong, direct flight. Their loud, raucous calls are familiar sounds in Australian gardens and woodlands, where they dominate nectar sources.

children.name: Anthochaera carunculata
children.description: A large, noisy honeyeater with grey-brown plumage flecked with white, a red eye, and distinctive red wattles on the nape, common across southern Australia. It is an aggressive nectar feeder that dominates flowering eucalypts and banksias, driving off smaller birds with loud, guttural calls. It also consumes insects and berries, and its distinctive coughing alarm call is a fixture in Australian gardens.

children.name: Phylidonyris
children.description: A genus of boldly patterned honeyeaters with white eyebrows and prominent black facial markings, found in coastal and heathland habitats across southern Australia and New Guinea. Their large, brush-tipped tongues are highly specialised for nectar extraction from tubular flowers such as banksias and grevilleas. They are active, restless foragers that often feed in noisy flocks.

children.name: Phylidonyris novaehollandiae
children.description: A boldly patterned black-and-white honeyeater with a distinctive white eyebrow, a yellow wing-patch, and a piercing, metallic call, widespread across southern Australia. It is one of the most common honeyeaters in heathland and urban gardens, where it visits flowers year-round with remarkable energy. It is highly territorial around flowering shrubs during the breeding season.

children.name: Manorina
children.description: The miners — a genus of medium-sized, highly social honeyeaters endemic to Australia. They are known for their complex social organisation, living in large, sedentary colonies that cooperatively defend territories. Their calls are varied and harsh, and their behaviour is often aggressive toward other bird species, particularly around food sources and nest sites.

children.name: Manorina melanocephala
children.description: A grey bird with a black crown and a bright yellow bill, known for its aggressive, colonial behaviour and loud, discordant calls, abundant in eastern Australia. Noisy Miners form large, cooperative colonies that aggressively exclude other bird species from their territory, significantly reducing local avian biodiversity. They are now one of Australia's most common urban birds.

children.name: Manorina melanophrys
children.description: A small, olive-green miner with a yellow-orange bill and legs, famous for its distinctive single, bell-like 'tink' call that echoes through the forests of eastern Australia. Bell Miners live in dense, cooperative colonies in riparian vegetation, where their presence is strongly associated with psyllid insect infestations. They feed almost exclusively on the sugary lerps produced by these sap-sucking insects.

children.name: Prosthemadera
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to New Zealand, containing the Tui — a large, charismatic honeyeater with distinctive white throat tufts. It has a uniquely ornate, double-voiced songbox that allows it to produce complex, layered vocalisations. Tuys were an important food source for Mori and feature prominently in Mori culture and ornithological lore.

children.name: Prosthemadera novaeseelandiae
children.description: A striking, iridescent green-black honeyeater with a distinctive white tuft at the throat, endemic to New Zealand. Its complex, melodic song incorporates both original notes and mimicry of other birds, and it is one of the most accomplished songbirds in the region. The Tui is an important pollinator of native New Zealand plants, particularly the flax and kowhai.

children.name: Entomyzon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Blue-faced Honeyeater, a large, strikingly plumaged honeyeater of northern and eastern Australia and New Guinea. Its most distinctive feature is the large patch of bare, bright blue skin around the eye, which intensifies during breeding. It is a bold, adaptable species that readily visits bird feeders and is a favourite in suburban gardens.

children.name: Entomyzon cyanotis
children.description: A large, unmistakable honeyeater with a black crown, white nape, olive-green back, and a vivid patch of bare blue skin surrounding the eye, found across northern and eastern Australia and southern New Guinea. It is bold and inquisitive, often approaching humans at picnic areas and feeding stations. It feeds on nectar, fruit, and insects, and is known to take eggs and nestlings of smaller birds.
